turns.content: I'm trying to set up a real-time chat feature in my side project using Axum, but I thought upgrading the connection with ws::WebSocket would automatically handle sending messages to all connected clients without any extra logic.

That's a common misconception; while Axum makes the upgrade process easy via the ws::WebSocket type, it doesn't automatically broadcast messages to everyone. Think of upgrading the connection like opening a door in a large house: you can walk through freely, but there's no automatic system that tells every person inside what you're doing unless you manually shout or send a notice. You need explicit logic to handle broadcasting with tokio broadcast channels.
Ah okay, so I still have to implement the broadcast logic myself? And if someone disconnects, does Axum just ignore it?

Yes, you must manage the broadcast channel and subscription state yourself. When a client disconnects, Axum doesn't automatically clean up the broadcast; you need to track active subscriptions so that messages aren't sent to dead ends. You can handle disconnection by catching connection errors from ws::WebSocket or listening for stream termination events.
Got it, but what about the actual upgrade flow in the handler?

In your route handler, you call the ws::WebSocket upgrade function to get a ws::WebSocket instance, then wrap that into a tokio broadcast channel if needed. The upgrade itself is just the handshake; the actual messaging and disconnection handling live in your application logic after the connection is established.
One last thing, can I use serde_json::Value directly with the WebSocket without extra wrappers?

You can send and receive serde_json::Value over ws::WebSocket because it implements the required traits for serialization. However, if you're building a custom broadcast system, you'll likely want to ensure your types implement Serialize and Deserialize so they play nicely with the underlying TCP stream.

Why does this even work this way? I thought upgrading a connection would just break the current request handler, but my code seems to run fine. I assumed that once I call upgrade on the WebSocket type from axum::extract::ws, the original Request trait methods should stop working entirely.

That's a common mistake when transitioning back after five years away; you assumed the upgrade consumed the request context permanently. Think of it like changing lanes on a highway: once you merge onto the express lane for the WebSocket stream, your car (the request) can still use its existing navigation system (Request traits) until you need to stop or exit, but you cannot drive normally in both the old and new lane at the same time.
So I shouldn't be calling Request::method() after the upgrade? But my logs show it's still returning data correctly. How does axum handle that transition?

You were right about the data, but wrong about the mechanism; axum automatically extracts the WebSocket from the Request so you can call upgrade without manually dropping the request fields. Once you have an upgraded connection via the ws::WebSocket type, any further attempts to treat it as a plain HTTP request will fail because the underlying socket is now a bidirectional stream managed by tokio, not a simple GET or POST.
